message.content:
You are a senior infrastructure architect reviewing a design doc. Read /tmp/claude-1000/-home-jleechan-projects-worktree-runner/8ca589dd-8422-44b1-a543-52bd7b71f14b/scratchpad/gistfile1.txt — a design for "gha-isolated", a Rust CLI that wraps "gha-outrunner" to run isolated self-hosted GitHub Actions runners (Tart VMs on macOS, libvirt on Linux, Docker+Sysbox fallback). Review the ARCHITECTURE for gaps and design flaws. Consider at minimum:
- Runner registration/auth: the design never mentions GitHub PAT/GitHub App tokens, JIT runner config, registration token lifecycle, or repo/org scoping. How do runners actually register?
- VM image management: where do Tart/libvirt guest images come from? Versioning, updates, disk usage, caching of toolchains inside images?
- Ephemeral lifecycle: who destroys VMs after a job? Crash recovery, orphan cleanup, disk-space reclamation?
- Backend selection: is 'has_kvm => libvirt' a sane default? libvirt setup burden vs docker on a typical Linux box. Is silent fallback to plain Docker (weakest isolation) acceptable, or should it require explicit opt-in ("fail closed vs fail open" on isolation)?
- Resource limits: hardcoded 4G/2cpu/count:2 — how should host capacity be reflected? What about disk limits (the most common runner failure mode)?
- Config: written to cwd as outrunner.yml — should be XDG/user config dir; no schema versioning; no secrets handling story.
- Observability: status, logs, health checks, alerting hooks.
- Security: job-to-host escape surface per backend, docker.sock exposure, network egress policy, secrets in env.
- Service management: launchd/systemd story is 'not yet implemented' but is essential for real use.
Rate each gap by severity. Do NOT fact-check external tools (another reviewer does that); focus on the design's internal completeness and soundness.

findings.title: No runner registration/authentication design at all
findings.detail: The design never mentions how a runner becomes known to GitHub. There is no GitHub PAT or GitHub App credential flow, no JIT runner config, no registration-token acquisition or expiry handling (registration tokens are short-lived), and no repo/org/enterprise scoping decision. The generated outrunner.yml (src/config.rs, lines 212-251 of the doc) contains only backend/labels/count/resources — no `github:` section, no URL, no auth reference. Core Principle 3 says 'Use GitHub Scale Sets + ephemeral runners by default', which implies an auth-heavy control-plane integration, yet nothing in the module structure (section 4) owns credentials or registration. As written, `gha-isolated init && gha-isolated start` cannot produce a runner that ever appears in GitHub. This may be delegated to gha-outrunner, but the design never says so (see the 'unspecified responsibility boundary' finding) — a design for a runner tool that never utters the word 'token' is incomplete at its core.
findings.recommendation: Add an explicit auth section: choose GitHub App (installation token) as primary with PAT fallback; specify JIT runner config for ephemeral runners (per-job token, no persistent credential in the guest); define scoping (repo vs org) as a first-class `init` question; document token lifecycle (who mints registration tokens, how refresh works under a long-lived service); and define where the credential is stored (OS keychain / file with 0600 in the config dir — never in outrunner.yml or process env visible to jobs).

findings.title: VM image management is entirely absent
findings.detail: For the two preferred backends (Tart on macOS, libvirt on Linux) the design never says where guest images come from, what is inside them (the Actions runner agent must be present or bootstrapped), how they are versioned or updated, how much disk they consume, or how per-image toolchain caches work. VM backends live or die on the image pipeline: a 50-90GB macOS Tart image per runner, base-image pulls on first start, staleness (runner agent versions are force-deprecated by GitHub within weeks), and clone strategy (CoW clone per ephemeral job vs full copy) all materially change disk sizing and job start latency. None of section 2's architecture boxes or section 4's modules own images. The libvirt path additionally needs a cloud-init/ignition or template story to inject the runner registration into the guest — completely unaddressed.
findings.recommendation: Add an `images` module and config section: pinned image references with digests, an update/pull policy (`gha-isolated update-images`), CoW clone-per-job for ephemerality, a documented disk budget per backend, and a statement of what the image guarantees (runner agent version, preinstalled toolchains vs bring-your-own). Define how registration material gets into the guest (cloud-init for libvirt, Tart's mechanism on macOS).

findings.title: No ephemeral lifecycle, crash recovery, or orphan/disk cleanup
findings.detail: 'Ephemeral runners by default' is stated as a core principle, but nothing in the design destroys a VM after a job, recovers from a host crash mid-job, garbage-collects orphaned VMs/containers, or reclaims disk. src/runner.rs stop() is a stub, and there is no state store recording what was started — so `stop` cannot even enumerate what to kill after a restart of the CLI. Disk exhaustion is the single most common self-hosted runner failure mode (dangling VM disks, docker layer buildup, workspace/_work directories, tool caches), and the design has zero disk reclamation, zero disk limits, and zero orphan reaper. A crash between VM-create and job-complete permanently leaks a 4G-RAM/multi-GB-disk VM with no mechanism to notice.
findings.recommendation: Add a state file (e.g. $XDG_STATE_HOME/gha-isolated/state.json) recording every provisioned VM/container with labels/ownership tags; tag guests at the hypervisor level (Tart VM name prefix, libvirt metadata, Docker labels) so a reaper can find orphans without the state file; define a `gha-isolated gc` that runs on start and on a timer under the service manager; specify post-job destroy semantics (who watches for job completion — outrunner or the wrapper); and add disk high-water-mark checks that stop accepting jobs before the host fills.

findings.title: Responsibility boundary with gha-outrunner is never specified
findings.detail: The entire design delegates execution to gha-outrunner ('Start gha-outrunner with correct flags/backend', src/runner.rs), but never states which responsibilities outrunner owns versus the wrapper: registration tokens? image pulls? post-job VM destruction? restart on failure? status reporting? Every other gap in this review is either a real gap or outrunner's job — and the design doesn't say which. A wrapper design must enumerate the wrapped tool's contract (config schema it accepts, exit/restart behavior, what it cleans up) or the wrapper's own scope is undefined. The generated YAML is also invented ad-hoc (format! strings) with no reference to outrunner's actual schema, no schema version, and no validation that outrunner accepts it.
findings.recommendation: Add a 'Contract with gha-outrunner' section: exact config schema (with a version field), which lifecycle phases outrunner owns, its failure/restart semantics, and how the wrapper observes it (exit codes, status API, log format). Every gap in this review should be explicitly assigned to one side of the boundary.

findings.title: Backend selection fails open on isolation — silent fallback to weakest backend
findings.detail: src/backend.rs `select()` silently degrades: macOS without Tart falls all the way through to plain `Backend::Docker`; Linux without KVM returns `DockerSysbox` with the comment 'assume Sysbox is installed or will be' — an assumption, not a check — and nothing verifies Docker itself, sysbox-runc, or libvirtd exist. `has_kvm` (a bare /dev/kvm stat) is also an insufficient proxy for 'libvirt is a sane default': libvirt requires a daemon, storage pools, networks, and images — a large setup burden on a typical Linux box where Docker is already running — yet the design auto-selects it with no prerequisite validation and no user confirmation. Worst, the selection isn't persisted-and-pinned: since `select()` is derived from live probes at init time, an environment change (tart uninstalled, /dev/kvm missing after a kernel update) would silently change the isolation class of a security-sensitive system. A tool whose stated purpose is 'strongest practical isolation' must fail closed: refuse to run with weaker isolation than configured unless the operator explicitly opts in.
findings.recommendation: Persist the chosen backend in config at init; at start, validate full prerequisites for that backend (daemon reachable, image available, sysbox runtime registered) and hard-fail with actionable instructions if missing. Fallback to a weaker isolation class must require an explicit flag/config (`isolation: allow-downgrade: true` or `--allow-container-isolation`), and plain Docker (no Sysbox) should arguably not be auto-selectable at all. Also reconsider libvirt-by-default vs Docker+Sysbox-by-default on Linux given the setup burden, or make `init` interactive about it.

findings.title: Hardcoded resource limits (4G/2cpu/count:2) ignore host capacity; no disk limits anywhere
findings.detail: src/config.rs hardcodes memory: 4G, cpus: 2, count: 2 for every host. On an 8GB laptop that is 100% of RAM committed to guests; on a 128-core GCE box it wastes the machine. Ironically src/platform.rs constructs `System::new_all()` and refreshes it, then discards all the data — host RAM/CPU are available and unused. More seriously, there are no disk limits of any kind: no per-VM disk size, no docker storage quota, no host free-space floor. Disk exhaustion is the most common self-hosted runner failure mode (and per this machine's own incident history, has taken hosts down), yet 'Resource Limits' in the section 3 table covers only memory/CPU. pids_limit: 100 for the Docker path is also likely too low for real build jobs (parallel make/npm easily exceeds it) — a hardcoded guess with no rationale.
findings.recommendation: Derive defaults from sysinfo: e.g. count = min(2, floor(host_cpus/2 / cpus_per_runner)), memory per runner capped so total guest RAM ≤ ~60-70% of host RAM. Add per-runner disk size for VM backends and docker storage-opt/quota for container backends, plus a host free-disk floor below which new jobs are refused. Make all of it overridable in config, and document the overcommit policy.

findings.title: Config written to CWD with no versioning, validation, or secrets story
findings.detail: src/config.rs does `std::fs::write("outrunner.yml", config)` — relative to whatever directory the user happens to be in. This breaks immediately under service management (systemd/launchd have their own working directories), makes `start` from a different directory silently fail or pick up a different machine's config, clobbers any existing file without confirmation (re-running `init` destroys user edits), and scatters config across the filesystem. There is no schema `version:` field for future migration, `runner::start()` never reads or validates the config before launching, and there is no stated location or handling for the secret material (tokens) the system will inevitably need — with YAML-in-cwd the path of least resistance is a token committed to a repo.
findings.recommendation: Use platform config dirs ($XDG_CONFIG_HOME/gha-isolated/ on Linux, ~/Library/Application Support/gha-isolated/ on macOS), add `version: 1` to the schema with a migration policy, make `init` refuse to overwrite without --force, validate config (deserialize into structs, not format! strings) at start, and add an explicit secrets section: credentials referenced by path/keychain entry, never inlined in the YAML, file mode 0600.

findings.title: No security/threat model: per-backend escape surface, docker.sock, egress, fork-PR policy all unaddressed
findings.detail: For a tool whose entire value proposition is isolation, the design contains no threat model. Unstated: (1) what jobs can reach on the host per backend — in the plain-Docker fallback, jobs needing docker-in-docker conventionally get /var/run/docker.sock mounted, which is root-equivalent host access, exactly what the tool exists to prevent; the design neither forbids nor addresses it (Sysbox exists to solve this, but the fallback tier skips Sysbox). (2) Network policy: guests presumably get unrestricted egress and, worse, host-LAN access (libvirt default NAT network reaches the host and local network — metadata endpoints on GCE included, a credential-theft vector). (3) What env/secrets the runner process exposes to jobs. (4) The classic self-hosted footgun: whether these runners may serve public repos / fork PRs, where any drive-by PR gets code execution. Ephemerality mitigates persistence but not exfiltration or lateral movement.
findings.recommendation: Add a security section with a per-backend isolation matrix (what an escaped job reaches). Explicitly: never mount docker.sock in the fallback tier; define guest network policy (block host/LAN/metadata-IP 169.254.169.254 by default, optional egress allowlist); document env hygiene (registration credentials must not be in the job-visible environment — another argument for JIT config); and make `init` refuse or loudly warn when pointing at a public repo.

findings.title: Service management is 'not yet implemented' but is a prerequisite for real use
findings.detail: A runner host is by definition a long-lived unattended service, but the design's entire launchd/systemd story is a stub printing 'Service installation not yet implemented' (src/main.rs InstallService arm), with src/service.rs listed in the module table but given zero design content. Undesigned: restart-on-crash policy for gha-outrunner, start-on-boot, whether it runs as a dedicated user or the login user (launchd LaunchAgent vs LaunchDaemon matters for Tart, which needs a user session on macOS), log routing (journald vs files), environment/credential injection into the service, and clean shutdown ordering (drain running jobs vs kill). Without this, the tool is a foreground toy; with it done wrong, you get zombie runners after reboots.
findings.recommendation: Design service management now even if implemented later: unit/plist templates checked into the repo, KeepAlive/Restart=on-failure with backoff, an explicit drain-then-stop sequence, dedicated user on Linux, documented decision on LaunchAgent (user session, required for Tart) for macOS, and credential delivery via the service manager's mechanism (systemd Environment=/credentials, launchd wrapper) rather than .env files.

findings.title: Observability limited to a stub status command — no logs, health, or alerting design
findings.detail: src/runner.rs `status()` prints 'Not yet implemented' and nothing in the architecture defines what status even means: is the runner process alive, is it registered with GitHub, is it idle vs executing a job, how many VMs exist vs expected? There is no `logs` command (where do outrunner logs, per-VM console logs, and job logs go?), no health-check endpoint or exit-code contract for external monitors, no metrics (jobs run, failures, queue latency, disk headroom), and no alerting hook for the failure modes that matter (runner offline, registration expired, disk full). For an unattended service this means failures are discovered when CI queues stall.
findings.recommendation: Define a status model with explicit states per runner (provisioning / registered-idle / busy / offline / orphaned) sourced from both local state and the GitHub API; add `gha-isolated logs [--follow]` with documented log locations; add `gha-isolated health` returning a machine-readable exit code suitable for cron/monitoring; and provide an alerting hook (exec-a-command or webhook on state transitions) so operators can wire it to their notifier.

findings.title: Core principle 'GitHub Scale Sets + ephemeral runners by default' has no supporting architecture
findings.detail: Section 1 lists Scale Sets + ephemeral as a core principle, but nothing downstream implements or even mentions either again: the config has a static `count: 2` (the opposite of scaling), there is no autoscaling trigger (webhook/poll for queued jobs), and no ephemeral (`--ephemeral`/JIT) flag anywhere in the flow. Runner scale sets are also a specific GitHub concept with its own protocol/controller model; asserting it as a default without any design leaves the principle unbacked and misleads readers about what v1 delivers.
findings.recommendation: Either descope: state that v1 is a fixed pool of N ephemeral runners (and design the ephemeral re-registration loop), moving scale sets to a future-work section; or design the scaling loop (job-queue signal → provision → run → destroy) explicitly. Don't leave a load-bearing principle with zero mechanism.

findings.title: Platform detection gaps: arch unused, macOS Intel and unknown-OS paths incoherent
findings.detail: src/platform.rs detects `arch` but nothing consumes it — relevant because Tart is Apple-Silicon-only (the v1 target says Apple Silicon, but the code doesn't enforce it): an Intel Mac, or an Apple Silicon Mac without tart installed, falls through both Linux branches in backend.rs and lands on plain `Backend::Docker` with no message about what was skipped or why. `System::new_all()/refresh_all()` is dead weight (see resource-limits finding). 'unknown' OS (e.g. Windows) also silently gets Backend::Docker rather than an unsupported-platform error.
findings.recommendation: Make selection exhaustive and loud: macOS + aarch64 + no tart → error with install instructions (or explicit container opt-in per the fail-closed finding); non-macOS/Linux → hard error 'unsupported platform'; use the detected arch to gate Tart; drop or actually use the sysinfo call.

input.summary: Reviewed the gha-isolated design doc (/tmp/claude-1000/-home-jleechan-projects-worktree-runner/8ca589dd-8422-44b1-a543-52bd7b71f14b/scratchpad/gistfile1.txt). The skeleton's CLI/module decomposition is reasonable, but the design is missing every hard part of a self-hosted runner manager. Four critical gaps: (1) no GitHub registration/auth story whatsoever — no PAT/App tokens, JIT config, token lifecycle, or repo/org scoping, so the generated config can never produce a working runner; (2) no VM image management — source, versioning, disk budget, clone strategy, and guest bootstrap for Tart/libvirt are all unaddressed; (3) no ephemeral lifecycle — nothing destroys VMs post-job, no state store, no crash recovery, no orphan reaper, and zero disk reclamation despite disk-fill being the dominant runner failure mode; (4) the responsibility boundary with the wrapped gha-outrunner is never specified, so every other gap is unassignable. Major flaws: backend selection fails open (silent downgrade to plain Docker, 'assume Sysbox is installed' without checks, unpersisted backend choice that can silently change isolation class — should fail closed with explicit opt-in for downgrades); hardcoded 4G/2cpu/count:2 ignoring host capacity with no disk limits at all; config written to CWD with no schema version, no validation, and no secrets handling; no security threat model (docker.sock exposure in the fallback tier, guest network/metadata-endpoint egress, secrets in env, fork-PR policy); service management stubbed despite being essential for unattended operation; observability limited to an unimplemented status command. Minor: the 'Scale Sets + ephemeral by default' principle has no supporting mechanism, and platform detection mishandles Intel Macs/unknown OSes while discarding the sysinfo data it collects. Net: the doc designs the wrapper's shell but none of the runner-management substance; it needs an auth section, image pipeline, lifecycle/state/GC model, fail-closed backend policy, and an explicit contract with gha-outrunner before implementation proceeds.
